nodes.label: Marketing Mix Modeling
nodes.description: A privacy-friendly, highly resilient, data-driven statistical analysis that quantifies incremental sales impact and ROI of marketing activities. It is an econometric model aiming to quantify incremental impact on a pre-defined KPI such as sales or visits. Analytical methodology that measures effectiveness of marketing spend and performance across various channels. It determines optimal budget allocation mix and identifies where dollars are or are not being effective. An analytical approach used to measure different marketing channels and determine the value of the entire media mix. It is resilient to privacy changes and helps optimize monthly budget allocations. A statistical technique used to analyze the impact of various marketing activities on sales or conversion. It involves balancing predictive accuracy with business interpretability and is a core domain for the Robyn package. A statistical or Bayesian model used to understand marketing impact. XP results should inform priors moderately rather than fixing them, allowing for model disagreement. A statistical technique used to link marketing spend with Key Performance Indicators (KPIs). It is the central focus of the document and encompasses concepts like Adstock and Saturation. A multi-dimensional problem used to estimate unknown parameters such as coefficients, adstock, and saturation. It is the core subject of the document series and involves Bayesian approaches for uncertainty quantification. A statistical analysis method that evaluates marketing spend against business outcomes to determine the impact of different channels on target metrics. A methodology for analyzing the impact of marketing channels on revenue. In this context, it is implemented using a Bayesian approach with PyMC. A statistical technique used to analyze channel contributions to a target metric like revenue. It requires data preparation, model specification, and evaluation of test sets. A statistical technique used to analyze the impact of marketing activities on sales, requiring broad inputs like macroeconomic indicators and seasonality data. Holistic, econometric models measuring the incremental impact of marketing and non-marketing activities on a given Key Performance Indicator. It has existed since the 1950s and is growing in popularity due to its privacy-safe nature using aggregated data. Decades-old measurement technique evaluating marketing effectiveness across ad publishers. It relies on statistical relationships between media variables and outcomes to allocate budget. A statistical analysis technique quantifying the impact of marketing activities on sales using historical data and accounting for channel interactions. A statistical analysis of historical data to determine the impact of marketing spend, evolving from backward-looking justification to forward-looking optimization. A technology providing a holistic view of marketing's true incremental impact across various channels. It is used by GetYourGuide to understand channel ROI and cost per acquisition within an uncertain environment. Standard analytical approach for evaluating the effectiveness of marketing spend across different channels. A statistical model estimating the effect of marketing spend on revenue or new customer acquisition, often referred to as Media Mix Modeling. A statistical technique used to understand the impact of various marketing activities on a target variable such as sales. It is central to analyzing media mix contributions and temporal dynamics in marketing data. A scientific method used to measure and allocate marketing mix. It is a capability of the Cassandra platform and is used to estimate incremental returns rather than just attribution credit. Marketing Mix Modeling is a technique that estimates channel impact and replaces guesswork with data-driven attribution. It is the broader category of which the Bayesian Media Mix Model is a specific type. A data-driven approach used to identify key drivers of business outcomes like sales or revenue. It focuses on analyzing the effectiveness of advertising spend across different media channels. A statistical technique used to isolate the true causal contribution of marketing channels at a channel level. It controls for organic baseline, seasonality, and cross-channel effects.

nodes.label: Key Performance Indicator
nodes.description: The dependent variable in the model, such as sales, website visits, or account sign-ups. It represents the primary outcome measured against which marketing impact is assessed.

The organization hosts measurement partner websites and develops open-source code for forecasting. Meta serves as a researcher partner and program sponsor for the study. It hosts the Meta MMM badged partner program which provided survey participants. Meta is a major technology company that provides advertising platforms and tools for businesses. It offers Conversion Lift Tests directly on its platform and handles test management, monitoring, and post-test analysis for advertisers. Technology company that developed the Robyn codebase and publishes case studies via Meta Open Source. It serves as the publisher of the BARK Marketing Mix Modeling case study document. The owner of the GeoLift library, providing tools for causal inference in marketing experiments. A technology company that developed the Robyn R package through its Facebook Experimental team. It also created the Nevergrad optimization platform and Prophet time-series forecast package. A technology platform formerly known as Facebook that provides tools like Conversion Lift Tests and GeoLift package. It is analyzed regarding cookie contamination threats. The organization behind Facebook Experimental Robyn which provides resources like the Analyst’s Guide to MMM. It contributes to the open-source state of marketing measurement tools. A technology company that developed the Measurement 360 framework and publishes content on Facebook Business News. It serves as the primary developer for this measurement strategy. An advertising platform including Facebook and Instagram, reported with billions of daily active users as of October 2024. A competitor used as a benchmark for YouTube/Video performance through Meta Performance advertising. A major technology company providing advertising platforms like Meta Ads Manager. It utilizes a Calibration Methodology encouraging advertisers to use conversion lift results.

nodes.description: A specific initiative by the Meta Marketing Science team to build in-house models. It is part of the broader Robyn product suite and available on GitHub for open-source use. An organization that provides the fictitious dataset used in the PyMC MMM demo notebook for educational purposes.

nodes.label: Robyn
nodes.description: Open-source R code using machine learning techniques for in-house or DIY modelers. It provides end-to-end scripts for modeling, including feature engineering and optimization. An open-sourced machine-learning based package for Marketing Mix Modeling developed by Meta to support calibration experiments. An open-source Marketing Mix Modeling (MMM) package. It includes a budget allocator for optimizing media spend. Open source Marketing Mix Modeling solution developed by Meta's marketing science team to democratize access to best-in-class MMM. It enables cross-channel measurement and analysis of ad spend inputs from digital and traditional channels. Facebook's open-source MMM initiative that features automatic tuning of hyperparameters and embedded lift results. It was initially brand-new but later evolved to include calibration and granularity in version 2.0. An R package designed for Marketing Mix Modeling, Time-Series Forecasting, and Multi-Objective Optimization. It is developed by Meta's Facebook Experimental team and released in version 3.12.0 as of December 2024. An open-source package from Facebook Experimental focused on MMM modeling. It recommends using media exposure metrics like impressions or GRPs for modeling. Open source Marketing Mix Model (MMM) package from Meta. It is used to evaluate marketing effectiveness and allocate budget, with specific versions like v3.8 introducing new calibration features. A competitor or alternative MMM library developed by Meta, compared with PyMC-Marketing methodology. A Bayesian programming framework developed by Facebook for marketing mix modeling and trend decomposition. An open-source tool associated with Meta that serves as an option for Marketing Mix Modeling, available via Facebook Research. Robyn is Facebook's open source MMM tool that provides a dataset and framework for analysis. It is licensed under the MIT License and supports various media channels.

nodes.label: Ridge Regression
nodes.description: A regularization technique used to reduce variance at the cost of bias, preventing overfitting and multicollinearity. It is implemented in Robyn using the glmnet library. A regularised regression method using L2-form regularization to handle multicollinearity. It is preferred in Robyn over Lasso because it avoids eliminating features, which is unrealistic for marketing mix modeling. A regression model used to estimate coefficients and parameters in Marketing Mix Modeling. It is implemented using the Scikit-Learn library.

nodes.label: Prophet
nodes.description: Meta open-source code for forecasting time series data. It is included in Robyn to decompose data into trend, seasonality, holiday, and weekday impacts. A time-series forecast package created by Meta that reduces human bias in modeling. It is used within Robyn to decompose trend, season, holiday, and weekday components automatically. Library used to decompose trends, seasonality, and holidays from data before modeling. A forecasting library by Facebook referenced for its usage of Fourier modes to capture seasonality effects. A tool used to decompose trends, seasonality, and holidays from time series data. It helps in preparing data for Marketing Mix Modeling analysis.

nodes.label: Nevergrad
nodes.description: Meta's evolutionary optimization platform used by Robyn for semi-automated model selection. It performs multi-objective optimization to select optimal model results automatically. Meta's gradient-free optimization platform used for Multi-Objective Optimization within Robyn. It employs evolutionary algorithms like TwoPointsDE to balance prediction and interpretability.

nodes.label: Meta Conversion Lift
nodes.description: A people-based incrementality study methodology used to calibrate MMM coefficients. It serves as ground truth for validating the accuracy of marketing impact studies.

nodes.label: Meta GeoLift
nodes.description: A geo-based incrementality study methodology used to calibrate MMM models. It provides ground truth data for validating model coefficients against actual lift.

nodes.label: John Wanamaker
nodes.description: Historical figure quoted regarding wasted advertising spend, stating half the money spent on advertising is wasted. His quote motivates the need for MMM to identify effective spending.

nodes.label: Analyst's Guide to MMM
nodes.description: A comprehensive document summarizing the methodology and tools for Marketing Mix Modeling. It serves as the primary source for the knowledge graph construction.

nodes.label: Privacy-friendly
nodes.description: A key strength of MMM that overcomes signal loss by not needing user-level data. It runs off aggregated or campaign-level data instead of individual user tracking.

nodes.label: Signal-resilient
nodes.description: The ability to overcome signal loss compared to multi-touch attribution which relies on online signals. This attribute makes MMM robust against data quality issues.

nodes.label: Holistic
nodes.description: Measures sales outcomes for all marketing channels together in one analysis. It accounts for both marketing and non-marketing activities like price and seasonality.

nodes.label: Geometric Adstock
nodes.description: A simple transformation requiring one parameter theta with a fixed decay rate. It is recommended for TV channels and faster to run than Weibull functions. An adstock function that is easiest to understand and explain with fewest parameters. It is recommended for first-time practitioners entering the field of Marketing Mix Modeling.

nodes.label: Weibull CDF Adstock
nodes.description: A cumulative distribution function offering flexible decay rates with two parameters. It is recommended for digital channels and allows for shape and scale adjustments.

nodes.label: Hill Function
nodes.description: A two-parametric function in Robyn reflecting saturation of each media channel. It controls the shape of the curve between exponential and S-shape using alpha and gamma parameters. A functional form originating from pharmacology used to model shape effects. It includes parameters for half saturation point and slope, though identifiability issues can arise. A nonlinear saturation curve function used to model media spend response. It is parameterized by alpha and gamma. An S- or L-shape function used to model diminishing returns in saturation transformation. It calculates the saturated media response based on adstocked media and parameters like alpha and gamma.

nodes.label: Granular Data
nodes.description: Data collected at a detailed level, such as campaign attributes or impression data. Modeling with granular data improves accuracy and provides more actionable results.

nodes.label: Multicollinearity
nodes.description: A risk where two or more independent variables are highly correlated with each other. It makes it difficult to calculate the impact of individual variables in the model. A data attribute added to media variables in simulation to mimic real-life situations where variables are correlated.

nodes.label: Incremental Sales Impact
nodes.description: The specific effect of marketing activities on sales volume over time, distinct from baseline performance. It is a core metric quantified by the Marketing Mix Modeling process.

nodes.label: Marketing Mix
nodes.description: A framework consisting of Product, Price, Place, and Promotion first proposed by Borden in 1964. It is popularized by McCarthy in 1978 and forms the basis for traditional media mix analysis.

nodes.label: Bayesian Methods
nodes.description: A statistical framework used to estimate parameters and make inferences based on prior knowledge and observed data. In this context, it is applied to improve media mix modeling with carryover and shape effects. A statistical framework that allows explicit inclusion of assumptions or priors and runs simulations weighted by plausibility.

nodes.label: Markov Chain Monte Carlo
nodes.description: A class of algorithms for sampling from probability distributions based on Markov chains. It is used in Bayesian inference to approximate posterior distributions when analytical solutions are difficult. A family of sampling algorithms used for complex, multi-dimensional problems where no closed formula exists for the posterior. It is employed by PyMC to enable sampling from prior distributions. A computational method used to evaluate posterior distributions in Bayesian models. Algorithms like No-U-Turn Sampler and Metropolis sampling are commonly employed. An algorithmic method used in PyMC for sampling from the posterior distribution of a Bayesian model. It is essential for estimating parameters in MMM. An algorithm used for running huge numbers of simulations to keep plausible ones, often utilized in advanced implementations of Bayesian MMM. Markov Chain Monte Carlo is a method used for inference in Bayesian models. It requires correct convergence to ensure valid probability estimates and is central to the Bayesian Inference process.

nodes.label: Gibbs Sampling
nodes.description: An MCMC algorithm implemented in C++ using slice sampler and BOOM library. It is noted for being more efficient than other samplers when there is high correlation between transformation parameters.

nodes.label: Hamiltonian Monte Carlo
nodes.description: A sampling method used by the Stan Sampler that utilizes gradient information. It is generally slower on large data sets but provides robust sampling for complex posterior distributions.

nodes.label: Adstock Function
nodes.description: A transformation applied to media spend to model carryover effects. It accounts for delayed consumer response, inventory, or word-of-mouth occurring in time periods following the pulse of advertising. The Adstock Function is a transformation used to model decaying effects of advertising over time. It captures the lagged impact of impressions from channels like TV and Radio on conversions. A transformation that captures the carryover effect of marketing activities over time, extending impact beyond the immediate period. The Adstock Function models time-course effects such as short-term versus long-term impact decay. It is integrated into the Bayesian Media Mix Model to capture lagged responses.

nodes.label: Shape Effect
nodes.description: Non-linear response curves representing ad saturation and diminishing returns at high levels of spend. Traditional linear models fail to capture this effect effectively.

nodes.label: Curvature Function
nodes.description: An alternative functional form for modeling shape effects, including the Hill function and Reach transformation. It is used to capture non-linear response curves in media spend.

nodes.label: Return on Ad Spend
nodes.description: A metric calculated by setting spend to zero in a change period and comparing predicted revenue against historical spend. It measures the change in revenue per dollar spent including carryover effects. A key performance indicator representing revenue generated per unit of ad spend, which is the primary metric being predicted by the models. A metric defined as total response divided by total spend. It is a key target for the budget allocator scenarios. A metric for efficiency calculated via cost-per-unit conversion. It measures the revenue generated per dollar spent, allowing for fair comparison across channels with different native units. The reported metric (ROAS) from the advertising platform, which is systematically higher than true incremental ROI. The metric used to evaluate channel effectiveness. It is subject to bias without calibration and serves as a key performance indicator for marketing decisions. A known overall marketing metric that previously lacked granular information per channel for Negative Underwear. A key performance metric representing revenue generated per unit of ad spend. It is available as an attributed metric by platforms and a true incremental ROI metric for causal contribution.

nodes.label: Marginal Return on Ad Spend
nodes.description: A metric measuring additional revenue from one-unit increase in spend. It is calculated via derivative or numerical perturbation and helps assess the marginal impact of media channels.

nodes.label: Optimal Media Mix
nodes.description: The allocation of budget across media channels that maximizes revenue under a budget constraint. It involves optimizing the objective function across posterior samples or per sample.

nodes.label: Sales Response
nodes.description: The response variable in the model, typically sales or log-transformed sales. It is influenced by media spend, control variables like price, and noise terms.

nodes.label: Bayesian Information Criterion
nodes.description: A metric used for model selection that penalizes complexity. In the study, it favored the most parsimonious model despite similar log-likelihoods across different specifications.

nodes.label: Traditional Models
nodes.description: Usually linear regression models that infer causation from correlation. They often fail to capture lag effects, shape effects, and suffer from limitations in causal inference challenges.

nodes.label: Propensity Score Methods
nodes.description: Techniques used for causal inference that are hard to implement due to limited data. Correlated explanatory variables affect the closeness of correlation-to-causation in these methods.

nodes.label: Causal Inference Challenges
nodes.description: Difficulties in establishing causation from correlation, particularly when data is limited or explanatory variables are correlated. Traditional techniques struggle with these challenges.

nodes.id: Bayesian Methods for Media Mix Modeling with Carryover and Shape Effects
nodes.label: Bayesian Methods for Media Mix Modeling with Carryover and Shape Effects
nodes.description: A research document published by Google Inc. on April 14th, 2017. It details a Bayesian framework for media mix modeling including carryover and shape effects. A seminal paper from Google Research outlining the order of transformations in MMM. It discusses applying saturation then adstock versus adstock then saturation based on media spend patterns. A seminal paper by Google Research that defines modern-day MMMs. It is a key resource for understanding Bayesian flavors of the modeling methodology. A primary academic paper published in 2017 that provides the theoretical foundation for the model implementation. An academic paper published by Jin et al. providing foundational methods for MMM with carryover effects.

nodes.label: Shampoo Advertiser
nodes.description: The real data application case study used to test the proposed models. Data was sourced from Neustar MarketShare and consolidated from various media tracking sources.

nodes.label: Model Selection
nodes.description: Model selection is the process of choosing a final marketing mix model for use. It involves evaluating factors like business plausibility and consistency with previous models.

nodes.label: Calibrated Marketing Mix Model
nodes.description: A method that improves prediction accuracy of marketing mix models by constraining predicted response closer to Randomized Controlled Trials results.

nodes.label: siMMMulator
nodes.description: An open source package from Meta specifically designed for generating simulated MMM datasets and defining causal effects like true conversion rates.

nodes.label: ROBYN HACKATHON 2022
nodes.description: The first ever public hackathon for Robyn hosted by Meta Marketing Science Team to foster community development and innovation.

nodes.label: Calibration Simulation Project
nodes.description: A proof-of-concept project created during the hackathon to demonstrate MMM calibration capabilities using simulated data.

nodes.label: Randomized Controlled Trials
nodes.description: Data points generated based on simulated true lift used for calibration use cases to constrain predicted responses in models.

nodes.label: Mean Absolute Percentage Error
nodes.description: An evaluation metric computed between True ROAS and Estimated ROAS to measure model accuracy and serve as an objective function. Mean Absolute Percentage Error is an evaluation metric for model performance used during hyperparameter tuning. It is calculated using the mean_absolute_percentage_error function.

nodes.label: Contextual Calibration
nodes.description: A feature in latest Robyn versions that calibrates true ROAS against immediate effect instead of total effect for better performance. New feature in Robyn v3.8 that isolates immediate effect from carryover. It offers an edge over naïve calibration by addressing scope differences between MMM and experiments.

nodes.id: Decision-Making Processes in Marketing Mix Modelling: A Survey of MMM Providers
nodes.label: Decision-Making Processes in Marketing Mix Modelling: A Survey of MMM Providers
nodes.description: This is an exploratory survey article first published on October 12, 2023. It investigates the decision-making criteria used by marketing mix modelling providers for model selection and evaluation.

nodes.label: Marketing Mix Modelling Decomposition
nodes.description: This is a key output of Marketing Mix Modelling that details the attribution of outcomes to individual variables. It is typically visualized as a stacked chart showing absolute contributions over time.

nodes.label: Statistical Fit
nodes.description: Statistical fit is a measure of model quality often prioritized in traditional analysis. In this context, it is compared against business logic and plausibility criteria.

nodes.label: Business Plausibility
nodes.description: Business Plausibility refers to the evaluation of model results based on business logic and signs of coefficients. It is prioritized over statistical fit by respondents in the survey.

nodes.label: Growth Department
nodes.description: The Growth Department is an internal division at Qonto that utilizes data science teams to support marketing strategies. It relies on incrementality experiments to refine budget allocation and testing processes.

nodes.label: Data Science Team
nodes.description: The Data Science Team is a specialized group within Qonto that manages technical entities like attribution models and incrementality tests. They are responsible for implementing tools such as GeoLift and PyMC to support marketing measurement.

nodes.label: Attribution Model
nodes.description: An Attribution Model assigns conversions to specific marketing touchpoints, typically using a Last-click principle. It provides descriptive information but often overestimates certain channels and fails to measure incremental impact accurately. The Attribution Model measures contacts and conversions attributed by platform through non-causal association. It often shows high precision but with an inflated scale compared to incrementality tests.

nodes.label: Conversion Lift Test
nodes.description: A Conversion Lift Test is a type of incrementality test that randomizes based on individual users to measure causal impact. It involves splitting audiences into control and treated groups to compute lift and confidence levels.

nodes.label: Geo-based Experiment
nodes.description: A Geo-based Experiment is an incrementality test that randomizes based on geography, comparing treated regions against control regions. It provides full ownership of data but presents challenges in finding optimal regional splits.

nodes.label: Incrementality
nodes.description: Incrementality refers to the causal impact of a marketing channel measured by comparing conversion numbers between exposed and non-exposed groups. It is a key metric for determining the true effectiveness of advertising spend. The causal relationship between a marketing action and a result, which is the primary goal of the experiment. A metric or insight gained through test-and-learn approaches within the framework. It helps advertisers determine if their strategies are actually working. A metric that measures the true causal impact of marketing spend, distinguishing it from attributed conversions that would have occurred anyway. A concept defining the true, incremental impact of marketing on business outcomes rather than just correlation. It is a key metric used by GetYourGuide to evaluate the effectiveness of their media mix strategies. The causal impact of marketing spend on outcomes. This concept is central to the function of GeoLift by Recast and the validation of Multi-stage Media Mix Modeling. The true incremental impact of marketing efforts versus organic baseline demand. It is the core metric measured by Recast. Incrementality refers to the true impact of marketing efforts on sales or performance, distinct from organic growth. It is Lume's primary focus. The true performance of a media mix representing new customers generated by specific channels.

nodes.label: Cost per Incremental Conversion
nodes.description: Cost per Incremental Conversion is a metric used to adjust marketing budget allocation by accounting for the actual incremental impact. It helps establish lower bounds for cost efficiency in acquisition channels.

nodes.label: Minimum Detectable Effect
nodes.description: The Minimum Detectable Effect is a threshold for statistical significance that determines if an observed uplift is real or noise. Higher volumes allow for smaller MDE, distinguishing true impact from random variation. The threshold for statistical significance in testing. Relaxing requirements allows for multi-cell tests with lower volume data, which is crucial for high velocity scheduling.

nodes.label: Meta Pixel

nodes.label: Marginal ROAS
nodes.description: A metric that converges to equilibrium when constraints are relaxed, representing the efficiency of spend at the margin.

nodes.label: Marginal CPA
nodes.description: A metric that converges when optimization freedom increases, indicating cost efficiency per acquisition at the margin.

nodes.label: Alpha Parameter
nodes.description: A parameter controlling the shape of the Hill Function saturation curve. Recommended range is 0.5 to 3.

nodes.label: Gamma Parameter
nodes.description: A parameter controlling the inflexion point of the saturation curve. It helps adjust for overspending history or minimum reach.

nodes.label: Saturation Curve
nodes.description: The nonlinear response function derived from media spend, taking C-shape or S-shape based on the alpha parameter. The relationship between media spend and response, modeled via specific functions. It helps understand how effectiveness changes with increased investment.

nodes.label: target_efficiency Scenario
nodes.description: A scenario added in Robyn v3.10.1 that sets ROAS or CPA targets with a variable budget constraint for growth advertisers.

nodes.label: max_response Scenario
nodes.description: A scenario that maximizes response given a fixed total budget level, operating as a zero-sum game between channels.

nodes.label: Bayes' Theorem
nodes.description: The fundamental theorem for updating probabilities given evidence, consisting of likelihood, prior, and posterior components. It is central to the Bayesian approach discussed in the document.

nodes.label: Bayesian Statistics
nodes.description: A philosophy where probability expresses a degree of belief in an event that updates based on new evidence. It contrasts with frequentist philosophy and is used for uncertainty quantification in MMM.

nodes.label: Frequentist Philosophy
nodes.description: A statistical approach where probability is based on the frequency of events rather than degrees of belief. It serves as a contrast to Bayesian statistics within the context of marketing measurement.

nodes.label: Prior Distributions
nodes.description: Used to express uncertainty and variability in initial assumptions before seeing data. They influence the posterior distribution and are crucial for specifying a Bayesian model correctly. Statistical inputs used to provide clues to the model as prior knowledge. They are adapted into posterior distributions via exposure to data evidence.

nodes.label: Posterior Distribution
nodes.description: The updated probability distribution after considering new evidence or data. It represents the average of likelihood and prior in the Bayesian framework.

nodes.label: Likelihood
nodes.description: Represents the probability of evidence given a hypothesis, such as negative reviews given a favorite restaurant. It is a key component of Bayes' Theorem alongside prior and posterior.

nodes.label: Beta Distribution
nodes.description: A distribution suitable for conversion rates bounded between 0 and 1. It acts as a conjugate prior for the Binomial distribution in Bayesian modeling contexts.

nodes.label: Binomial Distribution
nodes.description: Represents the probability of k successes from n trials, such as clicks from visits. It is often paired with Beta distributions in conjugate prior setups for MMM.

nodes.label: Half Normal Distribution
nodes.description: A distribution bounded below by 0 used for channel coefficients like Paid or Organic effects. It ensures non-negative impact estimates in the regression equation.

nodes.label: Paid Channel Coefficients
nodes.description: Parameters included in the MMM regression equation representing the effect of paid channels. They are typically modeled with a Half Normal distribution to ensure non-negative effects.

nodes.label: Uniform Distribution
nodes.description: Defined by upper and lower bounds, used when undecided on the direction of impact. It is applied to competitor marketing investment when the benefit or harm scenario is uncertain.

nodes.label: Competitor Marketing Investment

nodes.label: Return on Investment
nodes.description: A key performance indicator that MMM helps measure, including overall and channel-specific ROI. It is a primary metric used to evaluate marketing efficiency and budget allocation. A key metric assessed by the model to determine the effectiveness of marketing channels. The optimal investment level is calculated analytically to maximize this return. Return on Investment is a metric calculated for budget allocation in marketing mix modeling. It is used to optimize media spend across different channels. A metric representing the effect of marketing spend on sales, which should generally not be negative in plausible models.

nodes.label: Sales

nodes.label: Model Evaluation
nodes.description: A process involving metrics like Confidence Intervals, Credible Intervals, R-Squared, and Root-Mean-Square-Error. It is essential for mitigating overfitting risk and validating model generalization.

nodes.label: Root-Mean-Square-Error
nodes.description: A genuine error metric used alongside R-Squared to evaluate model performance. It is preferably calculated on test-set values to avoid inflated variance explanations.

nodes.label: R-Squared
nodes.description: A metric indicating the proportion of variance explained by the MMM. It should be viewed alongside genuine error metrics like Root-Mean-Square-Error to avoid over-valuing.

nodes.label: Correlation vs. Causation
nodes.description: Argument that correlation does not imply causation. MMM is correlational while experiments are causal, creating a fundamental distinction in how they measure marketing impact.

nodes.label: Naïve Calibration
nodes.description: Approach that aims to drive MMM's total predicted effect closer to causal estimates using a Bayesian framework. It leads to underestimation of calibrated channels and suboptimal budget allocations.

nodes.label: Last Dollar vs. Average Dollar
nodes.description: Distinction between measuring last dollar impact (experiments) versus long-term average impact (MMM). Experiments measure the lower bound of a channel's effect while MMM measures holistic outcomes.

nodes.label: Some Outcomes vs. All Outcomes
nodes.description: Difference in scope where experiments cover a subset of matchable outcomes like online products, while MMM aims to measure outcomes holistically across online and offline sales channels.

nodes.label: ROAS Discrepancy
nodes.description: Metric showing a 25% difference observed in recent research between MMM and experiment estimates. This discrepancy highlights the need for calibration to align the two measurement methods.

nodes.label: Media Mix Modeling
nodes.description: Media Mix Modeling is a statistical technique used to quantify the effectiveness of advertising on business metrics like customer acquisition. It has been in use since the 1960s and aims to optimize budget allocation across channels. A solution to estimate the impact of channels between Sales and Marketing when there is no traceable link between a sale and the Marketing action. It serves as a descriptive tool rather than a predictive tool. A statistical technique used to measure the incremental impact of media channels. It replaced older in-platform metrics for attribution. A holistic measurement methodology used to assess marketing impact on new subscriber growth. It handles complex marketing activities and distribution channels effectively. Media Mix Modeling is a statistical technique used by Recast to address pace and breadth challenges in marketing measurement. A statistical technique used to understand how advertising channels contribute to business outcomes like sales or conversions.

nodes.label: PyMC3
nodes.description: PyMC3 is a Python library used for Bayesian statistical modeling. In this context, it is utilized to implement the MMM model, define priors, and sample posterior distributions. A probabilistic modeling library in Python used to implement prior distributions in the Bayesian Media Mix Modeling approach. It supports the statistical tools required for the analysis. Library used for Bayesian modeling in previous articles by the author to compare with machine learning approaches. A Python library that serves as an MCMC engine for running simulations and weighting plausibility in Bayesian models.

nodes.label: Cookies (Web-tracking)

nodes.label: Privacy Regulations

nodes.label: Funnel Effect
nodes.description: The Funnel Effect describes the phenomenon where upper-funnel marketing influences lower-funnel channels. It acknowledges that offline or brand awareness activities impact direct response metrics like search engine marketing.

nodes.label: Search Engine Marketing
nodes.description: Search Engine Marketing is a lower-funnel online channel used for direct response. It is influenced by upper-funnel channels and is often excluded from the core model to avoid double-counting effects.

nodes.label: Television (TV)
nodes.description: Television is an offline marketing channel typically used for brand awareness. It requires specific modeling techniques like adstock functions to capture its decaying impact over time.

nodes.label: Incremental Customer Acquisition Cost
nodes.description: Incremental Customer Acquisition Cost is a standard measure of marketing efficiency calculated as spend divided by estimated customers acquired. It helps evaluate the true cost of gaining new subscribers.

nodes.label: Constrained Optimization Algorithm
nodes.description: This algorithm is used to generate budget recommendations that maximize conversions. It takes model coefficients and constraints like total budget into account for strategic planning.

nodes.label: Classical Media Mix Modeling
nodes.description: A method using linear regression with nonlinear transformations for marketing features. It requires previous assumptions on media channel behavior to assess contribution and is contrasted with Bayesian approaches.

nodes.label: Hill Equation
nodes.description: A mathematical model used to describe the saturation effect, representing the nonlinear relation between investment and revenue. It includes parameters for half-saturation point and shape.

nodes.label: Adstock Equation
nodes.description: A mathematical model that describes revenue's time allocation after channel-specific investment, accounting for time-delay effects. It includes parameters for peak delay and retain rate.

nodes.label: PySTAN
nodes.description: A probabilistic modeling library in Python used to implement prior distributions in the Bayesian Media Mix Modeling approach. It supports the statistical tools required for the analysis.

nodes.label: Target Sales
nodes.description: The dependent variable in the regression equation representing sales outcomes. It is influenced by marketing investments and external control variables.

nodes.label: Marketing Investments
nodes.description: Independent variables representing investment on different Marketing actions. These are transformed using nonlinear functions like the Hill Equation to estimate impact.

nodes.label: External Control Variables
nodes.description: Factors outside Marketing that impact sales, such as pricing, competition, and seasonality. They are included in the model with linear and immediate transformations.

nodes.label: Saturation Effect
nodes.description: A nonlinear relation between investment and revenue where maximal revenue is reached at a certain point. The Hill Equation models this specific behavior. A non-linear relationship assumption where the impact of additional media spend diminishes as saturation is reached.

nodes.label: Time Delay
nodes.description: The phenomenon where revenue effects are observed after a delay relative to the marketing investment. The Adstock Equation models this time allocation.

nodes.label: MichaelisMentenSaturation

nodes.label: Low Budget Scenario

nodes.label: High Budget Scenario
nodes.description: An optimization scenario defined by a total budget of $10,000. In this regime, channels are deep into saturation curves, and additional spend yields almost no incremental response.

nodes.label: Efficiency Regime
nodes.description: A state where cheaper impressions result in higher efficiency per dollar, prompting the optimizer to shift budget toward social media channels. It is typically observed under low budget constraints.

nodes.label: Saturation Regime
nodes.description: A state where channels plateau and additional spend yields no incremental response. In this regime, the optimizer allocates more to social media if impressions are expensive to reach saturation.
